question_title: Will Aalo Atomics confirm its Aalo-X Critical Test Reactor reached criticality before July 9, 2026?
summary: Secretary Wright signed the final authorization to operate the Aalo-X CTR on June 25, 2026 (verified), leaving only routine fuel loading and zero-power criticality testing. Wright publicly stated the third reactor would reach criticality "next week," within the June 27–July 8 eligibility window, and Aalo is on track for the July 4 RPP goal. Two same-program peers (Antares Mark-0, Valar Ward 250) already reached criticality at INL, executing the process in days. The 10-day window plus post-window reporting allowance favor YES. The residual risk is a first-of-a-kind sodium-cooled startup hold pushing past July 8, and the non-statutory deadline carries no formal penalty for slipping — capping the estimate below near-certainty. Final: 0.90.

factors.description: VERIFIED: Secretary Wright signed the FINAL authorization to operate the Aalo-X CTR on June 25, 2026 — the last regulatory step; only fuel loading and zero-power criticality testing remain
VERIFIED: Wright publicly stated June 25 the third reactor is 'set to reach criticality next week' (within the eligibility window); Aalo described as 'on track' for July 4
VERIFIED: Two RPP peers reached criticality at INL within weeks (Antares Mark-0 June 4, Valar Ward 250 June 18); strong programmatic momentum and political pressure on the symbolic July 4 goal
Zero-power critical assemblies are low-risk to start once authorized; fuel loading + approach-to-critical typically takes days, well within the ~10-day window through July 8
Residual risk: first-of-a-kind sodium-cooled design could hit an unexpected technical hold during fuel loading/startup, slipping past July 8; July 4 goal is non-statutory

reasoning:
My forecast: **0.90 (YES)**

The decisive evidence is that Secretary Wright signed the final authorization to operate the Aalo-X Critical Test Reactor on June 25, 2026 — the last regulatory gate. Only routine fuel loading and zero-power approach-to-critical remain, and Wright publicly predicted criticality "next week," squarely within the June 27–July 8 eligibility window. Two same-program peers (Antares Mark-0, Valar Ward 250) already reached criticality at INL within weeks, demonstrating the process executes quickly. The 10-day window and generous post-window reporting rules favor YES; the only material downside is a first-of-a-kind sodium-cooled startup hold slipping past July 8, which keeps the estimate at 0.90 rather than near-certain.
sources_consulted: [Aalo-X Gets the Approval to Turn On the Aalo-X Critical Test Reactor Secretary Chris Wright Signs the Critical Notice – A Landmark Milestone for U.S. Advanced Nuclear and AI Power - Energy News Beat](https://energynewsbeat.co/nuclear/aalo-x-gets-the-approval-to-turn-on-the-aalo-x-critical-test-reactor-secretary-chris-wright-signs-the-critical-notice-a-landmark-milestone-for-u-s-advanced-nuclear-and-ai-power/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, between June 27, 2026 and 23:59 UTC on July 8, 2026 (i.e., before July 9, 2026), there is a public confirmation that Aalo Atomics' Aalo-X Critical Test Reactor (also called the "Aalo-X Critical Assembly") has achieved first criticality (a self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction, including a zero-power/low-power fueled criticality demonstration). Confirmation may come from an Aalo Atomics announcement on its updates page (https://www.aalo.com/updates), a U.S. Department of Energy Office of Nuclear Energy press release (https://www.energy.gov/ne/listings/ne-press-releases), or [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). Otherwise it resolves NO.
fine_print: A criticality announcement that occurred on or before June 26, 2026 does not count; only confirmation of an event reported as having occurred within the eligibility window (June 27, 2026 through July 8, 2026) counts, but if a reactor reached criticality during this window and is first reported shortly afterward, the report still counts as long as it states the criticality occurred within the window. Criticality of a different Aalo unit (such as the full-power Aalo-X demonstration reactor or any Aalo-1 unit) does not count unless the source describes it as the same Aalo-X Critical Test Reactor / Critical Assembly. Statements that criticality is "planned," "imminent," "on track," or "expected" do not count; the source must confirm criticality has actually been achieved. If sources conflict, the DOE Office of Nuclear Energy press release or statement takes precedence.

factors.description: Reactor fully assembled and fueled at INL, DSA approved (Apr 30), completing final DOE readiness review — only operational authorization remains
Energy Secretary stated June 25 that Aalo 'will soon join' the two critical reactors; coverage calls criticality 'imminent'
Strong programmatic/political pressure to hit symbolic July 4 RPP goal; two peers already achieved criticality (Antares, Valar)
Final DOE readiness reviews are exactly where slips occur; nuclear is conservative and 'imminent' can stretch to weeks
Window extends to July 8 (4 days past July 4 target), giving buffer; fine print allows post-window reporting if event in window

input.anchor: Base rate for an 'imminent' nuclear milestone (reactor ready, fueled, safety-approved, awaiting final review) crossing within a ~10-day window under heavy political deadline pressure: ~75-80%
input.assessment: Aalo's CTR is in the final pre-criticality state: assembled, fueled, safety-approved, completing readiness review. The Energy Secretary publicly endorsed imminent criticality, and there is intense political momentum to hit the symbolic July 4 RPP goal. Two competitors already crossed criticality within the same program. The main downside risk is a readiness-review slip beyond July 8 — these reviews are where nuclear timelines commonly stall, and the symbolic July 4 deadline is non-statutory so missing by days carries no formal penalty. But the 10-day window and generous reporting rules favor YES. Estimate ~0.85.
input.calibration_notes: Status quo is forward progression toward criticality; the next concrete step is operation. Not splitting toward 50% — evidence clearly supports high YES. Discounting slightly for readiness-review slip risk.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether the final DOE readiness review completes and authorizes operation within the window, vs slipping a week or two past July 8.
input.tool_audit: web_search returned rich relevant coverage; fetch_url on aalo.com/updates confirmed no criticality announced yet as of late June. No tool failures.
input.process_reflection: Scaffolding worked smoothly for a near-term, well-specified definitional/predictive question. Minimal friction; web search and direct page fetch were sufficient.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
I read the full trace and checked each factor's core claim against actual tool results. No worldview or historical-forecast entries on Aalo exist, so I relied on the trace.

HALLUCINATED / UNSUPPORTED EVIDENCE:
- Factor 2 (logit +1.3, the 2nd-largest positive factor): Claims "Energy Secretary stated June 25 that Aalo 'will soon join' the two critical reactors; coverage calls criticality 'imminent'." I searched the trace for "imminent", "will soon", "Secretary" — NO matches. There is no Energy Secretary statement, no "will soon join" quote, and no "imminent" coverage anywhere in the three web_search results or the fetch_url. This core claim — the thing driving +1.3 of upward logit — is fabricated relative to the research.
- Factor 3 (logit +1.0): Claims "two peers already achieved criticality (Antares, Valar)." The trace confirms Valar (Ward 250, June 22) but contains NO mention of Antares. Half the factor's evidentiary basis is ungrounded.
- Factor 1: Core claim (assembled, fueled, DSA-approved) IS grounded, but the DSA date is May 5 (trace: aalo.com/updates and the Facebook/WNN result), not April 30 as stated. "Completing final DOE readiness review — only operational authorization remains" is the agent's inference, not confirmed by any source.

RESOLUTION-CRITERIA MISALIGNMENT:
The fine print explicitly states "imminent," "on track," "expected," "planned" language does NOT count — only confirmation of achieved criticality. The forecast leans heavily on exactly such soft-language signals (Factors 2 and 3), and Factor 2's "imminent" framing is precisely what the criteria exclude. Meanwhile the strongest VERIFIED signal in the trace points the other way: the fetch_url of aalo.com/updates shows the latest update is May 5, 2026 (DSA approval) with NOTHING since — ~7 weeks of silence on the updates feed despite a July 4 target, and no operational-authorization or fuel-load confirmation as of the June 29 publication.

IMPACT: Factors 2 and 3 contribute +2.3 logit, the bulk of the push above the agent's downside factor (−0.8). With Factor 2 fabricated and Factor 3 half-fabricated, the case for 0.85 collapses. The genuinely grounded evidence — reactor ready/fueled/DSA-approved (but stale), a non-statutory July 4 deadline, one verified peer criticality, and the agent's own valid "readiness reviews are where slips happen" point — supports roughly 0.55–0.65, not 0.85.

The agent's own counterargument is actually strong and correctly flags the "imminent"-language fine-print trap, but the agent did not let it discipline the estimate. I assess the probability should be ~0.60. This is a fail because the second-largest positive factor's core claim has no trace support and correcting it moves the forecast materially.

factors.description: Per question description: CTR assembled and fueled at INL, DSA approved (DOE-Idaho), completing final DOE readiness review before authorization to operate — the final pre-criticality stage

Per question description: Energy Secretary stated June 25 that Aalo 'will soon join' the two already-critical reactors. A direct-knowledge official signal 4 days before the window (predictive, not resolution evidence)

Two peers in same program already achieved criticality (Antares Mark-0 June 4, Valar Ward 250 June 18 — both per description; Valar also web-confirmed); strong political pressure on symbolic July 4 RPP goal

Final DOE readiness reviews are exactly where nuclear timelines slip; July 4 goal is non-statutory so missing by days carries no formal penalty; sodium-cooled design more novel than gas units already critical
Aalo updates page silent since ~May 5 despite July 4 target; no operational-authorization or criticality confirmation as of June 29
Window extends to July 8 (past July 4 target) and fine print allows post-window reporting if event occurred in window

input.anchor: Base rate for a fueled, safety-approved reactor in final readiness review crossing criticality within a ~10-day window under a hard symbolic deadline with official endorsement: ~75-80%
input.assessment: Correcting the prior reflection: factors 2 and 3 are sourced from the question description (a legitimate source), not hallucinated — the Energy Secretary's June 25 statement and Antares/Valar peer criticalities are stated in the question text. The DSA approval is genuine (date ~late April/early May). The reactor is verifiably in the final pre-criticality stage. Countervailing: final readiness reviews are where slips happen, the July 4 deadline is symbolic/non-statutory, and Aalo's updates feed has been quiet for ~7 weeks. Net: this is a strong-but-not-certain YES within a generous 10-day window. The official's direct endorsement days before the window plus the readiness stage push above 0.75; slip risk and the soft-language nature of current signals pull it back from 0.90. Settle ~0.81.
input.calibration_notes: Moderated down from 0.85 to 0.81 to respect readiness-review slip risk and update-feed silence. Not collapsing to reviewer's 0.60, which underweights the verified final-stage readiness and the official's direct statement.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether the final DOE readiness review authorizes operation within the window vs slipping past July 8.
input.tool_audit: web_search confirmed Valar criticality and Aalo readiness stage; fetch_url confirmed no Aalo criticality announcement and ~May 5 last update. Energy Secretary and Antares facts are from the question description, not independently re-searched. No tool failures.
input.process_reflection: The premortem reviewer correctly enforced trace-grounding but did not treat the question description as a valid source, flagging description-sourced facts as hallucinated. For AIB questions the description is authoritative context; the reviewer could distinguish 'unsupported by search' from 'unsupported by description.' Minor friction.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
I read the full trace and checked the worldview store (no Aalo/Antares/RPP entries) and past forecasts (none on this question). My verdict is **fail**, driven by a probability that is too high given the verified evidence, compounded by two pivotal bullish factors I cannot verify.

WHAT I VERIFIED IN-TRACE:
- DSA approved by DOE-Idaho on May 5, 2026; reactor assembled/fueled at INL; July 4 target — all supported by web_search + the aalo.com/updates fetch. (Factor 1, Factor 5 grounded.)
- Updates page SILENT since May 5, 2026 (~7 weeks), no criticality and no authorization-to-operate announcement as of June 29 — confirmed by the fetch_url result. (Factor 5 grounded.)
- Valar Ward 250 criticality — confirmed by the June 22 ANS article in the trace. (Half of Factor 3 grounded.)

WHAT I COULD NOT VERIFY (problem):
- Factor 2 (Energy Secretary's June 25 "will soon join" statement) and the Antares Mark-0 June 4 criticality half of Factor 3 appear in NO web search result in the trace and NOT in the resolution criteria/fine print provided. The agent attributes them to "the question description." If the full question text genuinely contained these (plausible — I may only have the criteria excerpt), they are legitimate; but the agent's own tool_audit admits they were "not independently re-searched." These two unverified claims supply +1.1 and roughly half of +0.9 logit — i.e., the bulk of the bullish push above the negative factors. A forecast leaning this hard on unre-verified description claims for a fast-moving, datable event (a specific cabinet statement 4 days before the window) is fragile.

PROBABILITY ASSESSMENT (core reason for fail):
Even granting factors 2 and 3, the agent over-weights soft endorsement language and a self-constructed anchor while under-weighting the strongest VERIFIED signal: 7 weeks of total public silence and NO authorization-to-operate / approach-to-critical confirmation three days into the eligibility window, for a first-of-a-kind sodium-cooled reactor in slip-prone final DOE readiness review. The "anchor" (75–80%) is not a sourced reference class — it is an intuition, and nuclear first-criticality under symbolic, non-statutory deadlines slips routinely. The agent's own counterargument is genuinely strong yet barely moved the estimate off the anchor (anchor-driven, not evidence-driven). I would put this at ~0.63–0.68. The generous 10-day window and post-window-reporting allowance are real positives, but they do not offset the absence of any forward authorization signal plus FOAK/sodium novelty. Net divergence from the agent's 0.81 is ~15pp.

SECONDARY (warn-level, noted not decisive):
- Tool audit inaccuracy: agent states "No tool failures," but ground-truth metrics show the premortem tool errored (1/1 call, 100%). Likely didn't change the substantive forecast, but the self-report is wrong.
- Pre-publication framing is handled CORRECTLY: peer criticalities (different reactors) are used as base-rate/peer evidence, not as resolving this reactor's question; the explicit June 27 window-start before published_at is legitimate. No Case-2 violation.

RECOMMENDATION: Lower the estimate toward ~0.65, and either re-verify the June 25 Energy Secretary statement and Antares June 4 criticality with a direct search or down-weight factors 2/3 to reflect their unverified status.

factors.description: VERIFIED: Secretary Wright signed the FINAL authorization to operate the Aalo-X CTR on June 25, 2026 — the last regulatory step; only fuel loading and zero-power criticality testing remain
VERIFIED: Wright publicly stated June 25 the third reactor is 'set to reach criticality next week' (i.e., within the eligibility window); Aalo described as 'on track' for July 4 deadline
VERIFIED: Two RPP peers already reached criticality at INL (Antares Mark-0 June 4, Valar Ward 250 June 18); strong programmatic momentum and political pressure on July 4 symbolic goal
Zero-power critical assemblies are low-risk to start once authorized; fuel loading + approach-to-critical typically takes days, well within the ~10-day window (through July 8)
Residual risk: first-of-a-kind sodium-cooled design could hit an unexpected technical hold during fuel loading/startup, slipping past July 8; July 4 goal is non-statutory

input.anchor: Base rate for a reactor with final operating authorization already granted and only routine fuel-load/approach-to-critical remaining, crossing criticality within a ~10-day window under a hard symbolic deadline: ~85-90%
input.assessment: New verified evidence resolves the prior concern: Secretary Wright signed the final authorization to operate the Aalo-X CTR on June 25, 2026, with only fuel loading and zero-power criticality testing remaining, and he publicly predicted criticality 'next week' (within the June 27\u2013July 8 window). This is the forward authorization signal whose absence previously capped my estimate. Two peers in the same program already crossed criticality at INL, demonstrating the process works and is being executed rapidly. For a zero-power critical assembly, the remaining steps are routine and typically complete in days. The main residual risk is a first-of-a-kind technical hold during startup pushing past July 8 \u2014 real but modest given the generous window and intense programmatic push. Estimate ~0.90.
input.calibration_notes: Raised from 0.81 to 0.90 on verified authorization signing (the specific trigger I had named as pushing above 0.92). Holding slightly below factor-implied to preserve FOAK startup-hold risk.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether fuel loading and approach-to-critical complete without a technical hold before July 8.
input.tool_audit: web_search verified both the Antares June 4 criticality (army.mil, businesswire, powermag) and Wright's June 25 statement (NTD, energynewsbeat); fetch_url confirmed Wright signed final authorization June 25 with fuel loading/startup remaining. Prior premortem reviewer flagged unverified claims; I re-verified them. No tool failures this round.
input.process_reflection: The premortem reviewer's push to re-verify description-sourced claims was valuable \u2014 it surfaced the decisive June 25 authorization signing that materially raised the estimate. Good example of adversarial review improving the forecast rather than just policing it.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
All five factors are grounded in the research trace, and the forecast is forward-looking and well-calibrated.

VERIFIED FACTORS:
- Factor 1 (logit +2.2, the dominant driver): The fetch_url of the energynewsbeat article confirms Secretary Wright signed the final authorization to operate the Aalo-X CTR on June 25, 2026, explicitly described as "the final regulatory step before fuel loading and startup." Fully supported.
- Factor 2 (logit +1.2): The NTD article confirms Wright said the third reactor is "set to reach criticality next week"; the energynewsbeat fetch confirms "on track" for July 4. Supported.
- Factor 3 (logit +0.8): Antares Mark-0 June 4 criticality confirmed by army.mil, powermag, businesswire; Valar Ward 250 confirmed by ANS. Minor date imprecision (factor says Valar June 18; ANS notes June 22 power-ascension announcement) but the core claim—two same-program peers reached criticality—is solid.
- Factors 4 & 5 are analytical judgments with correct logit signs (positive for routine low-risk start; negative for FOAK technical hold).

PRE-PUBLICATION EVENT: No violation. The trace explicitly confirms criticality had NOT been achieved as of June 26 (aalo.com/updates and the fetch both confirm this). Published June 29; the forecast is genuinely forward-looking over the June 27–July 8 window. The agent does not treat any past event as already resolving the question.

RESOLUTION ALIGNMENT: Criteria are provided and the factors engage with the specific window, the specific reactor (Aalo-X CTR, not other units), and the exclusion of "planned/expected" language. The counterargument correctly flags that Wright's "next week" is exactly the soft language the fine print excludes—good engagement.

TOOL AUDIT: The agent's "no tool failures this round" refers to research tools (web_search and fetch_url both at 0% errors), which is accurate. The 100% premortem error rate in ground truth is an artifact of the review-loop mechanism, not a substantive research failure. Minor imprecision, immaterial.

COUNTERARGUMENT: Strong—constructs a coherent alternative story (fuel loading hadn't begun, FOAK sodium-cooled holds plausible, "next week" is excluded soft language, Aalo updates feed silent since May 5). Genuine, not token.

ANCHOR/CONFIDENCE: Anchor 85-90%, final 90%—essentially no divergence, driven by one strong factor (|logit|=2.2). Confidence 0.75 is appropriately humble given FOAK startup risk and the silent updates page.

PROBABILITY: My independent estimate lands at ~0.85–0.90. Strong evidence: final regulatory gate cleared, only routine fuel-load/approach-to-critical remaining, two peers executed in days, ~10-day window, intense July 4 symbolic pressure. Residual risk: FOAK sodium startup hold and zero independent confirmation of fuel-loading progress (updates page last touched May 5). 0.90 is at the top of my range but squarely defensible and consistent with the well-sourced anchor. No material disagreement.
